References [14]

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: ingestion: severe toxicity; there appears to be some implication of increased cancer incidence with chronic ingestion of nitrites.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{LiNO}_2$ (m.pt. $220^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) melts without decomposition and appears stable to $\sim 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; at $\sim 350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , it is reported to decompose slowly, and this increases rapidly with increasing temperatures. The product gases are oxides of nitrogen (toxic); see B. Disaster hazards.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {73.3}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \tag {73.4}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is not removed, the nitrite may be oxidized to nitrate $(\mathrm{LiNO}_3)$ .

(iii) Nitrites, like nitrates, are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [15-21]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ is permitted in food for human consumption; there appears to be some implication of increased cancer incidence with chronic ingestion of nitrites

(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ (m.pt. $281^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) appears to melt without onset of decomposition; decomposition is reported at $\sim 300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; with formation of oxides of nitrogen (toxic); this increases with increasing temperatures (see B. Disaster hazards)

# (74) NaNO

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen)viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {74.5.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \tag {74.5.2}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is not continuously removed, the nitrite may be oxidized to $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , i.e. to the nitrate

(iii) Nitrites, like nitrates, are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous

References [41-49]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: ingestion toxicity rating, severe; there appears some implication of increased cancer incidence with chronic ingestion of nitrites.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ (m.pt.440°C) melts without decomposition, but at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , thermally decomposes into oxides of nitrogen, and at higher temperatures, to nitrogen and oxygen as gaseous products (see: B. Disaster hazards).

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {75.5}
$$

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {I} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {75.6}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

In the temperature range $550 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and under oxygen, the conversion of $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ to $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ goes to completion; between $650 - 750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two salts interconvert (see above), $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ becoming increasingly unstable; above $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the nitrite decomposition: $2\mathrm{KNO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{N}_2 + {}^3 /_2\mathrm{O}_2$ goes to completion.

(iii) Nitrites, like nitrates, are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [20-29]

References [35]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiF, at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ ; NaF at m.pt. $(995^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure: i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [36-41]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiF: $848^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

KF: 856°C

Eutectic melting point:

$492^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $50 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ LiF

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [32]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {77.1}
$$

precision: in table 77.1

uncertainty: $\sim$ 10%

$$

(C = \text {mol} \% \text {LiF})
$$

uncertainty: $\sim 5.0\%$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiF, severe; KF, severe; inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiF, at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ ; KF at m.pt. $(856^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosion": i.e., explosive generating of steam due to bulk water "carry-over: and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [35-40]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiF: $848^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

NaF: $995^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

KF: $856^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

454°C, composition: 46.5 mol % LiF; 11.5 mol % NaF; 42.0 mol % KF

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {78.1}
$$

precision: in table 78.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2.0\%$

3. Thermal Conductivity (liquid) $\left( {\lambda }_{\ell }\right)$

Measurement method: modified parallel plate [75,76]

Equation:

$$
\lambda = a + b T + c T ^ {2} \tag {78.6}
$$

precision: in table 78.16

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 25\%$

Table 78.16. Parameters of equation (78.6), precision, and temp. range   

<table><tr><td>Composition</td><td>a x 102</td><td>-b x 103</td><td>c x 106</td><td>Precision</td><td>T range(K)</td></tr><tr><td>ternary eutectic(a)</td><td>5.8049</td><td>0.1444</td><td>0.1009</td><td>*</td><td>800-920</td></tr></table>

(a) LiF:NaF:KF::46.5:11.5:42.0 (mol %)

\* not estimated; graphical data

Table 78.17. Thermal conductivity of molten eutectic from equation in table 78.16   

<table><tr><td>T(K)</td><td>\( \lambda x10^4 \)(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td>T(K)</td><td>\( \lambda x10^4 \)(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>800</td><td>71.1</td><td>870</td><td>87.9</td></tr><tr><td>810</td><td>72.9</td><td>880</td><td>91.1</td></tr><tr><td>820</td><td>74.9</td><td>890</td><td>94.6</td></tr><tr><td>830</td><td>77.1</td><td>900</td><td>98.2</td></tr><tr><td>840</td><td>79.5</td><td>910</td><td>102.0</td></tr><tr><td>850</td><td>82.1</td><td>920</td><td>106.0</td></tr><tr><td>860</td><td>84.9</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The containing material was Inconel. An analysis of the eutectic prior to the measurements showed the following impurity content initially (by weight, as metals): total, $0.13\%$ ; by trace metal content: Fe, $0.055\%$ ; Al, $0.012\%$ ; Ni, $0.009\%$ ; Pb, $0.014\%$ ; Mn, $0.010\%$ ; Mg, $0.008\%$ ; and "others", $0.002\%$ . On completion of the measurements, the above showed little change, except that chromium had increased to $\sim 0.12\%$ .

The thermal conductivity was observed to decrease with prolonged contact of the molten eutectic with Inconel. After cycling the above melt approximately three times between the limits $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ over a period 6-10 days, the $\lambda$ values were $20 - 30\%$ lower. The thermal conductivity, over the same temperature range, was given by:

$$
\lambda = 1. 7 7 2 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} - 4 0. 8 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} T + 3 2. 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 9} T ^ {2} (7 8. 7)
$$

The uncertainty limits are $\sim 25\%$ , the precision could not be estimated, since the results were reported graphically. The decrease in radiant transmission is presumably due to the solution of an absorbing species in the dissolution of the Inconel by the molten fluoride mixture.

References [75-82]

References [30,31]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiF at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ ; CaF $_2$ at m.pt. $(1418^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) \sim 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosion": i.e., explosive generating of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [32-38]

Table 79.5. Corrosion studies from primary research literature

$$

$$

$$
\left(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{BaF}_{2}\right)
$$

precision: in table 80.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mm}$ ; BaF₂, at m.pt. $(1320^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosion": i.e., explosive generating of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [24-29]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 80.3. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td>Studies (various molten fluorides)</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td>Cr</td><td>[31]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Cr-Fe</td><td>[32,33]</td></tr><tr><td>INOR-8</td><td>[33-36]</td></tr><tr><td>Al</td><td>[37,38]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Mo-Cr-Fe (Hastelloy)</td><td>[39,47,48]</td></tr><tr><td>Inconel</td><td>[36]</td></tr><tr><td>Cb-Zr; Ta-W-Zr-Cr; Ta-W-Cb</td><td>[49.50]</td></tr><tr><td>Corrosion: molten fluorides (survey)</td><td>[41-43]</td></tr><tr><td>Electro-chemical behaviour of oxide ions and related species in molten fluorides</td><td>[44]</td></tr><tr><td>Electro-analytical studies in molten fluorides</td><td>[45]</td></tr><tr><td>Annotated corrosion biblio.</td><td>[40,46]</td></tr></table>

Compatibility studies: various molten fluorides, and principally with molten LiF. No studies specific to $\mathsf{BaF}_2$ were found. For thermodynamic (theoretical) considerations, and some considerations relative to commercial grade fluorides, and the use of "getter" to lower impurity levels, see [46].

References [30-50]

$\mathrm{Cl}^{3+}$ , $\mathrm{U}^{4+}$ , $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$

precision: not estimated

uncertainty: in table 80.4

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiF: $848^{\circ}C$

$\mathrm{AlF}_3$ : does not melt; sublimes with 1 atm equal press. at $\sim 1255^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Eutectic melting point:

E1: 710°C, composition: 85.5 mol % LiF

E 2: $709^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 64.5 mol % LiF

![](images/1c43bb7434e7d3a42d10af950159aa43971cc63cbcac0f8c09bf7a11f0b8012c.jpg)  
Figure 81.1. LiF-AlF $_3$ phase diagram

2. Density (p)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [30,34]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {81.1}
$$

precision: in table 81.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiF at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ ; AlF₃ (m.pt. 1291°C), sublimes $\sim 1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [48-54]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaF: $995^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{MgF}_{2}$ : $1263^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

E 1: $830^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $25 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgF}_{2}$

E 2: $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $64 \, \text{mol} \, \% \, \text{MgF}_2$

Compound: $1030^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $50 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgF}_{2}$

![](images/aef79905fe31678b65fd29a78879c6e4421da406606b626edce3f25466208c0b.jpg)  
Figure 82.1. NaF-MgF $_2$ phase diagram

References [1-16]

2. Density (ρ)

No data

3. Surface Tension (γ)

No data

4. Viscosity (η)

No data

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaF at m.pt. $(995^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; MgF $_2$ at m.pt. $(1263^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) < 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure: i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [17-22]

Compatibility studies with various molten fluorides, including NaF and $\mathsf{MgF}_2$ . No compatibility studies specific to mixtures of molten NaF- $\mathsf{MgF}_2$ found.

System 83 $\mathrm{CaF}_2$ -NaF

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaF: $995^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{CaF}_2$ : $1418^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

818°C, composition: 32.5 mol % CaF₂

![](images/33ad38bfd9015ee4b1a4f712a4282a407850606a2304547261ac6baeb8fc08e5.jpg)  
Figure 83.1. $\mathrm{CaF}_2$ -NaF phase diagram

References [1-40]

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [41]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {83.1}
$$

precision: in table 83.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

Table 83.1. Parameters of equation (83.1) and precisions

References [16,19,42]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaF at m.pt. $(995^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; CaF $_2$ at m.pt. $(1418^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) \sim < 0.5\mathrm{mm}$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure: i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air   
(ii) Fluorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids

References [43-49]

References [25-27]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaF, at m.pt. (995°C), $\sim$ 0.5mm; BaF $_2$ , at m.pt. (1320°C) $\sim$ 0.5mm.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [28-33]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 84.5. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td>Studies</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td>Cr</td><td>[34]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Cr-Mo alloys (INOR-8; Hastelloysb, W, and N)</td><td>[35,36]</td></tr><tr><td>SSNI-12P</td><td>[37]</td></tr><tr><td>Quartz</td><td>[38]</td></tr><tr><td>Al</td><td>[39]</td></tr><tr><td>Various metals</td><td>[40]</td></tr><tr><td>Corrosion studies in molten salts with NaF as one component (e.g., Cl, CO3, ...)</td><td>[41-46,53,54]</td></tr><tr><td>Electro-chemical behaviour of oxide ions and related species in molten fluorides</td><td>[47-49]</td></tr><tr><td>Electroanalytical studies in molten fluorides</td><td>[50]</td></tr><tr><td>Annotated corrosion bibliio.</td><td>[51]</td></tr><tr><td>Corrosion: molten fluorides (survey)</td><td>[52]</td></tr></table>

Compatibility studies: various molten fluorides and principally with molten NaF. No studies specific to $\mathrm{BaF}_2$ were found. For thermodynamic (theoretical) considerations, and some considerations relative to commercial grade fluorides, and the use of "getter" to lower impurity levels, see [52].

NaF: 995°C

A1F.3: does not melt; sublimes with 1 atm equal press. at $\sim$ $1255^{\circ}C$

Eutectic melting point:

E. $888^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 15 mol % $\mathrm{AlF}_{3}$

$$

precision: in table 85.5 uncertainty: $\sim$ $10\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see NaF [78], and $\mathrm{AlF}_3$ [82].   
(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [76-82].

# 7. Corrosion

Table 85.9. Corrosion studies from primary research literature

A: studies principally in molten NaF, KF, and LiF;  
B: used largely in fluorides physical properties measurements; C: technological aspects, in aluminum reduction cells; D: more general studies, basic principles, and surveys

# 8. Diffusion

Measurement method: capillary

Diffusing species investigated in NaF-AlF₃ as solvent:

$\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ，Al(III)，F

precision: in table 85.10

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 10\%$

Equation:

$$
D = A \exp [ - E / R T ] \tag {85.5}
$$

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

KF: $856^{\circ}C$

$\mathrm{AlF}_3$ : does not melt; sublimes with 1 atm equal press. at $\sim 1255^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Eutectic melting point:

E1: 840°C, composition: 6.8 mol % AlF₃; [9.6 wt % AlF₃]

E₂: 570°C. composition: 4.5 mol % AlF₃; [54.2 wt % AlF₃]

P: $580^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition 47.5 mol % $\mathrm{AlF}_{3}$ ; [56.7 wt % $\mathrm{AlF}_{3}]$

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [22]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {86.1}
$$

precision: in table 86.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

(86) KF-A1F3

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see KF [29] and $\mathrm{AlF}_3$ [33]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [27-33]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiCl: $610^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

$557^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 77.5 mol % LiCl

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [27]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {87.1}
$$

precision: in table 87.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

Table 87.1. Parameters of equation (87.1) and precision

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight; NaCl, very slow.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiCl, at m.pt. $(610^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ $\sim 0.25\mathrm{mm}$ ; NaCl, at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic chloride fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [33-38]

\text {S y s t e m} 8 8 \quad \mathrm {L i C l - M g C l} _ {2}

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiCl: $610^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{MgC_12}$ ： $714^{\circ}C$

Eutectic melting point:

$570^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $60 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ LiCl

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {88.1}
$$

precision: in table 88.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

(88) LiCl-MgCl2

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight; $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ , slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiCl, at m.pt. (610°C), $\sim 0.11\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [22-27].

No compatibility studies specifically with molten $\mathrm{LiCl - MgCl_2}$ were found, for $\mathrm{MgCl_2}$ as a component in molten NaCl and KCl, see [32-36].

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiCl: 610°C

BaCl2: 962°C

Eutectic melting point:

512°C, composition: 70.7 mol % LiCl

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: modified maximum bubble pressure [21,22]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {89.1}
$$

precision: in table 89.1.

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2.0\%$

# References [25]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight; $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ , poisonous when taken orally.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiCl at m.pt. $(610^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.25\mathrm{mm}$ ; BaCl $_2$ , at m.pt. $(962^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) < 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic chloride fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [26-31]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 89.9. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td>Studies</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td>Mg, Ni, Zr, Ti</td><td>[32]</td></tr><tr><td>Ti, Zr, Hf, ThCl4</td><td>[33]</td></tr><tr><td>Armco Fe</td><td>[34-40°]</td></tr><tr><td>Zr</td><td>[41]</td></tr><tr><td>Solubility of metal oxides (Ni, Ca, Zn, Mg)</td><td>[32,42]</td></tr><tr><td>Thermodynamics of corrosion</td><td>[32,43,44]</td></tr><tr><td>Corrosion - annotated biblio.</td><td>[45]</td></tr><tr><td>Electrochemical aspects</td><td>[46]</td></tr><tr><td>Reviews: corrosion - molten salts</td><td>[47-49]</td></tr></table>

No compatibility studies specifically with molten $\mathrm{LiCl - BaCl}_2$ were found, for $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ as a component in molten NaCl and KCl, see [36-40].

References [32-49]

8. Diffusion   
No data   
9. Heat of Fusion $(\Delta H_{f}^{\circ})$ No data

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

MgCl2: 714°C

Eutectic melting point:

E i: $450^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 60.1 mol % NaCl

P1: 465°C, composition: 53.1 mol % NaCl

$\mathrm{P}_{2}$ : $485^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $64.7 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ NaCl

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [26]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {90.1}
$$

precision: in table 90.1 uncertainty: $\sim$ 2.0%

Table 90.1. Parameters of equation (90.1) and precisions

$$
\rho = a + b T + c T ^ {2} + d C ^ {2} + e C ^ {3} + f T C ^ {2} \tag {90.2}
$$

$$

$$

precision: in table 90.9 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2.0\%$

References [4,25,28,36-39]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; MgCl $_2$ , slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; MgCl $_2$ at m.pt. $(714^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.11\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.

References [40-45]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: 800°C

BaCl2: 962°C

Eutectic melting point:

$654^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $39 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [28]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {91.1}
$$

precision: in table 91.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

# 3. Surface Tension (γ)

Measurement method: maximum bubble pressure [33]

$$

$$

precision: in table 91.3 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 3.0\%$

References [27-29,34,36-38]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; $\mathsf{BaCl}_2$ , toxic (orally).   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm};$ BaCl2 at m.pt. $(962^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\sim <  0.5\mathrm{mm}.$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [39-45]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

KCl: $770^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

MgCl2: 714°C

Eutectic melting point:

E1: ~ 435°C, composition: 30 mol % MgCl2

E 2: ~ $435^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 36 mol % MgCl 2

E3: ~ 470°C, composition: 58 mol % MgCl2

![](images/cd3c99e1c599e8c1b0a640a61d78e47b29ef8aeca2b1eb84cbf1716ed1e464d3.jpg)  
Figure 92.1. KCl-MgCl $_2$ phase diagram

References [1-21]

2. Density (p)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [26]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {92.1}
$$

precision: in table 92.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

# (92) KCl-MgCl 2

# 3. Surface Tension (γ)

Measurement method: maximum bubble pressure [34]

Equation:

$$
\gamma = a + b T \tag {92.2}
$$

precision: in table 92.3

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2\%$

# (92) KCl-MgCl 2

# 4. Viscosity (n)

Measurement method: oscillating sphere technique [26]

Equation:

$$
\eta = a + b T + c T ^ {2} + d T ^ {3} \tag {92.3}
$$

precision: in table 92.5

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 15\%$

# 5. Electrical Conductance (K)

Measurement method: classical ac technique [47]

Equation:

$$
\kappa = a + b T + c T ^ {2} \tag {92.5}
$$

precision: in table 92.8

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 5.0\%$

References [22,25,28,30,42-48]

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: at m.pt $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.42\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ , at m.pt $(714^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ $\sim 0.11\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.  
References [1,49-53]

The $\lambda$ values for pure KCl reported in [86] are uniformly higher er ( $\sim 20\%$ ) than the recommended values [88]

Table 92.21. Isotherm of thermal conductivity at 973K   

<table><tr><td>Mol % MgCl2</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td>Mol % MgCl2</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>19.4</td><td>34</td><td>17.6</td></tr><tr><td>24</td><td>22.5</td><td>50</td><td>28.1</td></tr><tr><td>25</td><td>18.9</td><td>60</td><td>28.6</td></tr><tr><td>29</td><td>23.6</td><td>80</td><td>14.0</td></tr></table>

A number of metallurgical processes with KCl-MgCl $_2$ as a component take place at $\sim 700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , i.e. $\sim 973\mathrm{K}$ . The data (above) illustrate the thermal conductivity-composition isotherm at this temperature. In systems of nitrates not showing compound formation, the thermal conductivity generally shows a negative deviation from additivity (passing through a minimum at the equimolar composition).

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$$
\mathrm {C a C l} _ {2}: 7 8 2 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}
$$

$$
\mathrm {K C l}: \quad 7 7 0 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}
$$

Eutectic melting point:

E 1: $594^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $75.7 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ KCl

E2: 640°C, composition: 25.9 mol % KCl

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [30]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {93.1}
$$

precision: in table 93.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

$$
\rho = a + b C + c C ^ {2} + d T ^ {3} + e C ^ {3} + f C T ^ {2} \tag {93.2}
$$

$$
\left(\mathrm {C} = \mathrm {mol} \% \mathrm {CaCl}_{2}\right)
$$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: very low.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: KCl, at m.pt. $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) \sim 0.42\mathrm{mm}$ ; CaCl₂, at m.pt. $(782^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 2$ x $10^{-4}\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [50-55]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 93.8. Corrosion studies from primary research literature

References [56-77]

(93) KCl-CaCl2

8. Diffusion

Measurement method: capillary [78]

Diffusing species investigated in $\mathsf{KCl - CaCl}_2$ as solvent:

Ca 2+

precision: $\sim \pm 0.2\%$ uncertainty: $\sim \pm 10\%$

Equation:

D = 1.281 x $10^{3}$ exp [-7907/RT] (93.5)

Table 93.13. Isotherm of thermal conductivity at 1073 K   

<table><tr><td>CaCl2</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td>CaCl2</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>0</td><td>24.4</td><td>50</td><td>28.0</td></tr><tr><td>10</td><td>20.7</td><td>60</td><td>26.9</td></tr><tr><td>20</td><td>19.1</td><td>73</td><td>23.1</td></tr><tr><td>27</td><td>17.2</td><td>80</td><td>27.2</td></tr><tr><td>30</td><td>17.8</td><td>100</td><td>29.1</td></tr><tr><td>40</td><td>21.4</td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The data (above) illustrate the thermal conductivity-composition isotherm at this temperature. In systems of nitrates not showing compound formation, the thermal conductivity generally shows a negative deviation from additivity (passing through a minimum at the equimolar composition).

# 4. Viscosity (η)

No data

(94) $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ -KC1

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating [26-28,32]

(i) Toxicity: Soluble barium compounds, such as the chloride, are poisonous when taken orally.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see KC1 [28] and BaCl $_2$ [32]

B. Disaster hazards [29-31]

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [26-32]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

KC1: $770^{\circ}C$

CaCl2: 782°C

Eutectic melting point:

E1: 504°C, composition: 42 mol % NaCl, 6 mol % KCl

E2: 558°C, composition: 38 mol % NaCl, 31 mol % KCl

E3: 532°C, composition: 26 mol % NaCl, 50 mol % KCl

(95) KC1-NaCl-CaCl2

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; KCl, very low; CaCl $_2$ , slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt (800°C), ~ 0.34mm; KCl at m.pt. (770°C) ~ 0.42 mm; CaCl₂ at m.pt. (782°C) ~ 2 x 10⁴mm.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.

References [19-24].

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

KC1: $770^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

MgCl2: 714°C

Eutectic melting point:

$385^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $21.6 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{KCl}$ ; $33.0 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{NaCl}$ ; $45.4 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$

![](images/94973e92083671776dc3fca309feb35aa0f53ce4ec418a6fd555c11a342f246a.jpg)  
Figure 96.1. KCl-NaCl-MgCl $_2$ phase diagram

References [1-22]

2. Density (ρ)

No data

3. Surface Tension (Y)

No data

4. Viscosity (η)

No data

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

(96) KC1-NaCl-MgCl2

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; KCl, very low; MgCl $_2$ , slight.

(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ at m.pt. $(714^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.11\mathrm{mm}$ ; NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; KCl at m.pt. $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.42\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over: and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.

(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [23-28]

Table 96.1. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td>Studies</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td>Ni, Fe</td><td>[29]</td></tr><tr><td>Zr, Be, Ti, Hf, U</td><td>[30]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Cr-Fe</td><td>[31]</td></tr><tr><td>Ti</td><td>[32,33]</td></tr><tr><td>Cr2O3+ C electrode</td><td>[34]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe</td><td>[35]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Cr</td><td>[36]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe, Ni</td><td>[37]</td></tr><tr><td>Steel 3</td><td>[38]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni-Fe</td><td>[29,39]</td></tr><tr><td>Au, Pt, W</td><td>[40]</td></tr><tr><td>Several metals</td><td>[41]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe</td><td>[42]</td></tr><tr><td>Ni</td><td>[43]</td></tr><tr><td>Zr</td><td>[30]</td></tr><tr><td>Rh</td><td>[44]</td></tr><tr><td>Al</td><td>[45]</td></tr><tr><td>Cr, Fe-Cr</td><td>[46]</td></tr><tr><td>W</td><td>[47]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe, steels</td><td>[38,48,49]</td></tr><tr><td>Emf. series</td><td>[41]</td></tr><tr><td>Thermodynamic redox diagrams</td><td>[29,50]</td></tr><tr><td>Electrochemical approach</td><td>[51,52]</td></tr><tr><td>Reviews (molten salts corrosion)</td><td>[53-55]</td></tr><tr><td>Annotated corrosion biblio.</td><td>[56]</td></tr></table>

For studies with binary systems, see KC1-MgCl $_2$ [38, 48, 49]; NaCl-MgCl $_2$ [48]; NaCl-KCl [all except 38, 48, 49]. For compatibility studies with molten KC1-NaCl-MgCl $_2$ see [57]; corrosion is not a problem if storage salts are dried before use, and if mild steel is used as a container.

References [29-57]

\text {S y s t e m} 9 7 \quad \mathrm {C a C l} _ {2} - \mathrm {M g C l} _ {2}

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

CaCl2 782°C

$\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ ： $714^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

621°C, composition: 39 mol % CaCl $_2$

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [18]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {97.1}
$$

precision: in table 97.1 uncertainty: $\sim$ 1.0%

$$

$$
\gamma = a + b C + c C ^ {2} + d T ^ {3} + e T C ^ {2} + f C T ^ {2} \tag {97.3}
$$

$$
(C = \text {mol} \% \text {CaCl} _ {2})
$$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathsf{CaCl}_2$ , very low; $\mathsf{MgCl}_2$ , slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: at m.pt. $(782^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}$ ; $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ at m.pt. $(714^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.11 \mathrm{~mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over: and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolvehighly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [27-32]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

BaCl2: 962°C

Eutectic melting point:

E: $556^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $65 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$

P: $590 - 600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $60 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}$

2. Density (ρ)

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {98.1}
$$

precision: in table 98.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ , slight; $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ , toxic (orally).   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ at m.pt. (714°C) ~ 0.11mm; BaCl $_2$ at m.pt. (962°C), $\sim < 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [25-31]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points

KC1: $770^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{MgCl}_2$ ： $714^{\circ}C$

CaCl2: 782°C

Eutectic melting point:

E 1: $440^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 28 wt % KCl, 49 wt % MgCl 2, 23 wt % CaCl 2

E 2: $434^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 62 wt % KCl, 35 wt % MgCl 2, 3 wt % CaCl 2

$\mathrm{P}_{1}$ : $446^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 26 wt % KCl, 49 wt % MgCl $_2$ , 25 wt % CaCl $_2$

$\mathrm{P}_{2}$ : $437^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $59 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{KCl}, 38 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{MgCl}_{2}, 3 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: KCl, slight; $\mathsf{MgCl}_2$ slight, CaCl, slight.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: KCl at m.pt. $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.42\mathrm{mm}$ ; MgCl $_2$ at m.pt. $(714^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.11\mathrm{mm}$ ; CaCl $_2$ at m.pt. $(782^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 2 \times 10^{-4}\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [17-22]

# 7. Corrosion

BaCl2: 962°C

Eutectic melting point:

E: 594 C, composition: 36.5 mol % BaCl $_2$

P: 624 C, composition: $54 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$

References [23-25]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ , slight; $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ , toxic (orally).   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ at m.pt. (782°C), $\sim 2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mm}$ ; $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ at m.pt. (962°C), $\sim 0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.

References [26-32]

System 101 $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ : $253^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}$ : $307^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

$\sim 192^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $\sim 55 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ $\mathrm{LiNO}_{3}$

![](images/736f94dd5b398ee22ccefb1a183c5e9d38a93b7d8cff2d05edc64d5c1e84bd09.jpg)  
Figure 101.1. $\mathrm{LiNO}_3\text{-NaNO}_3$ phase diagram

References [1-19].

2. Density (p)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [20]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \quad (1 0 1. 1)
$$

precision: in table 101.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

Table 101.1. Parameters of equation ( 101.1) and precisions

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ , moderate; $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , permitted in food additive.   
(1i) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ decomposed just above its m.pt. (253°C) to the nitrite and oxygen; $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ (m.pt., 307°C), decomposition to nitrite onsets $\sim 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \rightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {I} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {101.6}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {101.7}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant. If the gas phase is not immediately removed, the NO may re-oxidize the nitrite to nitrate.

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds, oils, carbon;...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [26-31]

Table 101.9. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td>Studies in molten nitrates and nitrites</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td>Fe</td><td>[32-34]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe, Co, Ni, Cr, Al,...</td><td>[35-37]</td></tr><tr><td>Cu, Pt, Au, W,...</td><td>[36-38]</td></tr><tr><td>Zn, Pb, Cu, Ni, Al</td><td>[39]</td></tr><tr><td>Pt, S, steel</td><td>[40]</td></tr><tr><td>Zr</td><td>[41]</td></tr><tr><td>Oxide species</td><td>[42]</td></tr><tr><td>Electrochemical approach</td><td>[43,44]</td></tr><tr><td>Thermodynamic redox diagrams</td><td>[45,46]</td></tr><tr><td>Annotated corrosion bibliio.</td><td>[47]</td></tr><tr><td>Reviews/molten salts</td><td>[48-50]</td></tr></table>

The studies in Table 101.9 (above) relate to studies with various molten nitrates, principally $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , and their mixtures. No compatibility studies with molten $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ ; for studies in molten $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , see [34].

Saturation concentrations of the eutectic solid solutions : 9.1 mol % $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ and 1.7 mol % $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ ; heat of mixing of the eutectic solid solutions at 9.1 mol % $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ , 122 cal mol $^{-1}$ ; and at 1.7 mol % $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , 35 cal mol $^{-1}$ ; heat of mixing in the liquid state:

$$

$$

System 102 $\mathrm{LiNO}_3 - \mathrm{KNO}_3$

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ : $253^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

KNO3: 335°C

Eutectic melting point:

133°C, composition: 58.8 mol % KNO₃

![](images/d8d8be8d753a2339df45515d599698817b7569e5c7671a7a9ed8ea31a6dc8521.jpg)

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [21]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {102.1}
$$

precision: in table 102.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ , slight; $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , permitted as food additive.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ decomposes just above its m.pt. (253°C) to $\mathrm{LiNO}_2$ and oxygen. $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (m.pt. 335°C), decomposition similarly onsets $\sim 530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {102.8.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \quad (1 0 2. 8. 2)
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant. If the gas phase is not immediately removed, the NO may re-oxidize the nitrite to nitrate.

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon;...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [30-35]

System 103 $\mathrm{NaNO}_3 - \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{NO}_3)_2$

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaNO3: 307°C

Ca(NO3)2: 561°C

Eutectic melting point:

226°C, composition: 30 mol % Ca(NO₃)₂

# 6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , permitted as food additive; $\mathrm{Ca(NO_3)_2}$ , rated moderate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: Nitrates will decompose with heating to nitrites and oxygen; onset of decomposition: $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ (m.pt. $307^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\sim 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C};$ $\mathrm{Ca(NO_3)_2}$ (m.pt. $561^{\circ}\mathrm{C}),\sim \mathrm{m}.pt.$

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {103.5.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \quad (1 0 3. 5. 2)
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [23-28]

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , permitted as food additive; $\mathrm{Mg(NO_3)_2}$ , rated moderate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: Nitrates decompose, on heating, to nitrites and oxygen; onset of decomposition for $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (m.pt. $335^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ); is $\sim 530^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; little is known about the high temperature behavior of anhydrous $\mathrm{Mg(NO_3)_2}$ ; onset of decomposition has been reported from $\sim 130^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $\sim 390^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

$$
\mathrm {K N O} _ {3} - \mathrm {M g} \left(\mathrm {N O} _ {3}\right) _ {2} \tag {104}
$$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": violent generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; sudden explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {I} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {104.2}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {104.3}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

In the temperature range $550 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and under oxygen, the conversion of $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ to $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ goes to completion; between $650 - 750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two salts interconvert (see above), $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ becoming increasingly unstable; above $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the nitrite decomposition: $2\mathrm{KNO}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{N}_2 + 3 / _2\mathrm{O}_2$ goes to completion.

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [18-23]

# References [17]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{LiNO}_2$ , severe; $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ , permitted in food; there appears to be some implication of increased cancer with chronic ingestion of nitrites   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no data for this system; but see: $\mathrm{LiNO}_2$ [28], and $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ [28]

# (105) $\mathrm{LiNO}_2\text{-NaNO}_2$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {105.2}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \tag {105.3}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is not continuously removed, the nitrite may be oxidized to $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , i.e., to the nitrate

(iii) Nitrites, like nitrates, are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides)

References [18-28]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ : $282^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{KNO}_2$ : $440^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

$\sim 228^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $65 \mathrm{Mol} \% \mathrm{NaNO}_{2}$

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [18]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \quad (1 0 6. 1)
$$

precision: in table 106.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

Table 106.1. Parameters of equation (106.1) and precisions

References [20]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ ; low; $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ , low; (permitted in food); there appears to be some implication of increased cancer with chronic ingestion of nitrites.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no data for this system; but see $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ [30] and $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ [30]

# (106) $\mathrm{KNO}_2 - \mathrm{NaNO}_2$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Ondecomposition, nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {106.6}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \tag {106.7}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is not continuously removed, the nitrite may be oxidized to $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , i.e., to the nitrate.

(iii) Nitrites, like nitrates, are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides).

References [21-30].

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: very caustic; attacks all body tissue.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see NaOH [69] and KOH [70].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Hydroxides react exothermically with water or steam; the aqueous solution is strongly caustic; attacks living tissue; dangerous.

References [19-24]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ : $1010^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Li3AlF6: 785°C

Eutectic melting point:

713°C, composition: 36 mol % Na₃AlF₆

No data

No data

4. Viscosity (n)

Measurement method: oscillational sphere [30]

Equation: (viscosity-composition isotherm @ 1273 K)

$$
n = 2. 7 3 8 7 - 3. 3 5 2 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} C + 2. 4 5 3 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} C ^ {2} \tag {108.1}
$$

precision: $\sim$ 2.5%

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2.5\%$

$$
\mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {A l F} _ {6} - \mathrm {L i} _ {3} \mathrm {A l F} _ {6} \tag {108}
$$

# 5. Electrical Conductance (K)

Measurement method: classical ac technique [32]

Equation:

$$
\kappa = a + b T + c T ^ {2} \tag {108.2}
$$

precision: in table 108.2 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 3.0\%$

References [32-38]

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{AlF}_6$ at its m.pt. $(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $<< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ . $\mathrm{Li}_3\mathrm{AlF}_6$ at its m.pt. $(785^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $<< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [39-44].

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiF: 848°C

LiOH: $462^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

$430^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $80 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ LiOH

References [1-15]

2. Density (p)

No data

3. Surface Tension (γ)

No data

4. Viscosity (n)

No data

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiF, severe; LiOH, very caustic and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiF at m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ ; LiOH at m.pt. $(460^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) \ll 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosion": i.e., explosive generating of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.   
(iii) Hydroxides, react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.

References [16-21]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight; LiOH, very caustic and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system;

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic chloride fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) Hydroxides react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.

References [19-25]

13. Thermal Conductivity (liquid) $(\lambda_{\ell})$

Measurement method: comparative technique; flat slab [45]

Composition: LiOH:LiCl::63:37 (mol %)

$$
\lambda = 6. 1 2 6 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} + 1. 9 1 0 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} T \tag {110.1}
$$

precision: insufficient data for estimate uncertainty: $\sim \pm 15\%$

Table 110.2. Thermal conductivity of melt from equation (110.1)   

<table><tr><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td></td><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>550</td><td>16.6</td><td></td><td>610</td><td>17.8</td></tr><tr><td>560</td><td>16.8</td><td></td><td>620</td><td>18.0</td></tr><tr><td>570</td><td>17.0</td><td></td><td>630</td><td>18.2</td></tr><tr><td>580</td><td>17.2</td><td></td><td>640</td><td>18.3</td></tr><tr><td>590</td><td>17.4</td><td></td><td>650</td><td>18.5</td></tr><tr><td>600</td><td>17.6</td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The preceding are for mixtures from reagent grade quality LiOH and LiCl. With commercial grade chemicals, the thermal conductivity for this molten mixture over the same temperature range may be expressed by:

$$
\lambda = 5. 1 6 2 5 x 1 0 ^ {- 4} + 2. 0 3 0 0 x 1 0 ^ {- 6} T \tag {110.2}
$$

The values of $\lambda$ for mixtures from reagent grade and commercial grades LiOH and LiCl are in close accord ( $\sim \pm 1\%$ ). The data set was insufficient for precision estimates; the uncertainty limits are established to be $\sim \pm 15\%$ .

References [45,46]

14. Thermal Conductivity (solid) $(\lambda_{s})$

Measurement method: comparative technique; flat slab [45]

Composition: LiOH:LiCl::63:37 (mol %)

$$
\lambda = 3. 7 2 8 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} - 1. 7 2 2 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} T \quad (1 1 0. 3)
$$

precision: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 15\%$

Table 110.3. Thermal conductivity of melt from equation (110.3)   

<table><tr><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td></td><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>320</td><td>31.8</td><td></td><td>440</td><td>29.7</td></tr><tr><td>340</td><td>31.4</td><td></td><td>460</td><td>29.4</td></tr><tr><td>360</td><td>31.1</td><td></td><td>480</td><td>29.0</td></tr><tr><td>380</td><td>30.7</td><td></td><td>500</td><td>28.7</td></tr><tr><td>400</td><td>30.4</td><td></td><td>520</td><td>28.3</td></tr><tr><td>420</td><td>30.0</td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr></table>

The preceding are for mixtures of reagent grade quality LiOH and LiCl; with commercial grade chemicals, the $\lambda$ values were found to be uniformly somewhat smaller ( $\sim 2 - 5\%$ ); the thermal conductivity equation over the same temperature range is:

$$
\lambda = 3. 6 4 5 5 x 1 0 ^ {- 3} - 1. 6 6 6 0 x 1 0 ^ {- 6} T \tag {110.4}
$$

The data set was insufficient for precision estimates; the uncertainty limits are estimated to be $\sim 15\%$ .

$$

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight, $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ , moderate   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiCl at m.pt. $(625^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\sim 0.25\mathrm{mm}$ , $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ decomposes with heating to the nitrite and oxygen; decomposition onsets just above m.pt. $(235^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic chloride fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {I} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {111.2}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {111.3}
$$

# (111) LiCl-LiNO3

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant. If the gas phase is not immediately removed, the NO may re-oxidize the nitrite to nitrate.

(iv) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds oils; carbon;...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [18-24]

(112) $\mathrm{LiCl - Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiCl, slight; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , very toxic; lethal dose (oral), 0.7 mg/kg.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiCl at m.pt. $(610^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.25\mathrm{mm}$ ; Li $_2$ CO $_3$ (m.pt. $723^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), dissociates on heating to the oxide and CO $_2$ ; CO $_2$ pressure at $730^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 10\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic chloride fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Lithium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.

References [17-22]

# References [18]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, very low; Na $_2$ CO $_3$ , moderate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system, but see NaCl [21] and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}$ [21]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) When bulk NaCl is heated at high temperatures, vapor is evolved which is particularly irritating to the eyes; when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.

- When heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decomposes to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e., react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are remarkably aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns).

References [19-24].

[40], reviews [41-43].</td></tr><tr><td>B</td><td>Si [44], Pt [45], Metals [46], Cu-Zn [47], Pt, Au, Ag, MgO [48-51], Pt, Rh, Pd, Ir (O2environment) [52], Au, Ag, Al2O3(N2atmosphere; Na2CO3-Na2O) [53-55], quartz, Porcelain, Ag, Pt, Ni (N2atmosphere; Na2CO3-Na2O) [56], boron nitride [51,57], β-alumina [57], Fe (Na2CO3-Na halides) [58], acid-base relationships [59-62], H2O hydrolysis reactions [63-65], molten carbonates: fuel cells, thermal energy storage, coal gasification [26,57,58,66-70]</td></tr></table>

$$

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , classified as strongly caustic.

(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ decomposes on heating to the oxide and oxygen; $\mathrm{CO}_2$ pressure at $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 1\mathrm{mm}$ .

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions"; i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.

(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.

(iii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Potassium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.

References [19-24].

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; NaOH, very caustic and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl, at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; NaOH, at m.pt. $(460^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\ll 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.   
(iii) Dangerous; reacts with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very strongly caustic; attacks living tissue.

References [18-23]

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{NaNO}_{3}$ : $307^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

297°C, composition: 95 mol % NaNO₃

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ ; permitted as food additives.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ (m.pt. $335^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) decomposes on heating to the nitrite and oxygen; decomposition onsets $\sim 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Inorganic chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.   
(iii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen)viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {116.4}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {116.5}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iv) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [20-26]

(117) NaCl-Na₂SO₄

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ , unknown; in general the toxicity of inorganic sulfates are those of the cations; in this case sodium (slight)   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; Na $_2$ SO $_4$ at m.pt. $(884^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 10^{-5}\mathrm{mm}$ ; while sulfates decomposed to oxides of sulfur on heating, Na $_2$ SO $_4$ is relatively stable in the range of m.pt.

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved   
(iii) Sulfates, when heated to decomposition, evolve $\mathsf{SO}_3$ , which in turn dissociates to $\mathsf{SO}_2$ and $\mathsf{O}_2$ ; toxic, aggressive fumes

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in food; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ , severe.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ at m.pt. $(1069^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.07\mathrm{mm}$ ; while sulfates dissociate on heating to oxides of sulfur, $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ is relatively stable.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.   
(iii) Sulfates, when heated to decomposition, evolve $\mathsf{SO}_2$ , which, in turn, dissociates to $\mathsf{SO}_2$ and $\mathsf{O}_2$ ; toxic, aggressive fumes.

References [21-26]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: KCl, very low; $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , moderate.

(ii) Vapor pressure: no information of this system; but see KC1 [22], and $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ [22]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) When heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decomposes to form alkali metal oxide; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e., react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues.

References [20-25]

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: KCl, very low; $\mathsf{K}_2\mathsf{CO}_3$ , .classified as strongly caustic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: KCl at m.pt. $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.42\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ decomposes on heating to the oxide and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ; $\mathrm{CO}_2$ pressure at $\sim 1\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Potassium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.

References [20-25]

# 3. Surface Tension (Y)

Measurement method: maximum bubble pressure [23,24]

Equation: (surface tension-temperature dependence) [23]

$$
Y = a + b T \tag {121.2}
$$

precision: in table 121.3 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

Equation: (surface tension-composition isotherm) [24]

$$
Y = a ^ {\prime} + b ^ {\prime} C + c ^ {\prime} C ^ {2} \tag {121.3}
$$

$$
(C = \text {mol} \% K C 1)
$$

precision: in table 121.4 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: KCl, very low; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ , severe.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: KCl at m.pt. $(770^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.42 \mathrm{~mm}$ ; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ at m.pt. $(1069^{\circ}\mathrm{C}) \sim 0.07 \mathrm{~mm}$ ; sulfates dissociate on heating to form oxides of sulfur; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ is relatively stable.

# (121) $\mathrm{KCl - K_2SO_4}$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides evolve highly toxic fumes when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids.   
(iii) Sulfates, when heated to decomposition, evolve $\mathrm{SO}_3$ , which, in turn, dissociates to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ ; toxic, aggressive fumes.

References [25-30]

E1: 442°C, composition: 10.2 mol % Li2CO3 [18.5 equiv. % Li2CO3]

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , very toxic; lethal (oral) dose, $0.7\mathrm{mg/kg}$ ; LiOH, very caustic and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiOH at m.pt. $(460^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $<< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ (m.pt. $732^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) dissociates, on heating, to form the oxide and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ; $\mathrm{CO}_2$ pressures at $730^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 10\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e. explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides, the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Lithium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.   
(iii) Hydroxides react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.

References [20-26]

System 123 $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3 - \mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$$
\mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3}: 7 2 3 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}: & 8 5 9 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} \end{array}
$$

Eutectic melting point:

$534^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $38.5 \, \text{mol} \, \% \, \text{Li}_2 \text{CO}_3$

![](images/08e5f8d72f04dbc3afbea83623b3b463b78d1d4509bc458762816f93d739e90a.jpg)  
Figure 123.1. $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3 - \mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ phase diagram

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , very toxic; lethal (oral) dose, $0.7\mathrm{mg/kg}$ ; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ , unknown; for an inorganic sulfate, the toxicity is generally that of the cation, i.e. in this case, lithium.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ (m.pt. $723^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) dissociates on heating to the oxide and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ; $\mathrm{CO}_2$ pressure at $730^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, \sim 10\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ (m.pt. $8/59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), decomposes to oxides of sulfur (aggressive and toxic fumes) just above its m.pt.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e. explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Lithium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.   
(iii) Sulfates when heated to decomposition, decompose, evolving $\mathrm{SO}_3$ , which in turn dissociates to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ (see above); toxic, corrosive fumes.

References [20-26]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3}: 7 2 3 \pm 3 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} \\ \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4}: 1 0 6 9 \pm 3 ^ {\circ} \mathrm {C} \\ \end{array}
$$

Eutectic 1, melting point:

$$
470^{\circ}C, \text {composition}: 60 \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}; 26.5 \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}; 13.5 \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}
$$

Eutectic 2, melting point:

$$
562^{\circ}C, \text {composition}: 55\mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}; 35\mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}; 10\mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}
$$

Peritectic melting point:

$$
565^{\circ}C, \text {composition}: 65.83 \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}; 34.17 \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3}
$$

Reciprocal Salt Pair Reaction:

$$
\mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} + \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} + \mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} + 5. 3 \text {k c a l e q u i v} ^ {- 1}
$$

![](images/2099a547c120348e8002e297dbeb2802d763cdf2412f24caa5a92d6f01cacd7d.jpg)  
Figure 124.1. Phase diagram for Li, K/CO₃, SO₄ system.

System $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{CO}_3 - \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ is the diagonal as marked; note the reciprocal salt pair reaction

$$
\mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} + \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} = \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {S O} _ {4} + \mathrm {L i} _ {2} \mathrm {C O} _ {3} + 5. 3 \text {k c a l e q u i v} ^ {- 1}
$$

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

Measurement method: classical ac technique [17]

Equation:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \kappa = 2. 6 4 0 3 - 7. 3 3 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2} C + 9. 9 1 9 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} C ^ {2} - 4. 4 7 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} C ^ {3} \tag {124.2} \\ \left(\mathrm {C} = \mathrm {mol} \% \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {SO} _ {4}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

precision: $\sim \pm 6.5\%$ uncertainty: $\sim \pm 3.0\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , classified as strongly caustic; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ ; the toxic qualities are those of the cation, lithium.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ (m.pt. $898^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) decomposes on heating to the oxide and oxygen.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissolution pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Potassium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.   
(iii) Sulfates, when heated to decomposition, decompose, evolving $\mathrm{SO}_3$ , which in turn dissociates to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ (see above); toxic, corrosive fumes.

References [18-24].

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2.0\%$

3. Surface Tension (Y)

No data

4. Viscosity (n)

No data

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaOH, very caustic and corrosive to all body tissue; Na $_2$ CO $_3$ , moderate toxicity.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ [22], and NaOH [23]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) NaOH: Hydroxides react exothermically with water or steam; the aqueous solution is strongly caustic; attacks living tissue; dangerous.

$\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}$ : When heated with $\mathrm{CO_2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decomposes to form alkali metal oxide; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns).

References [20-26].
