References [17]

3. Surface Tension (γ) No data   
4. Viscosity (η) No data   
5. Electrical Conductance (K) No data   
6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , classified as a strong caustic; KOH, very caustic and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: KOH at m.pt. $(360^{\circ}\mathrm{C})\ll 0.5\mathrm{mm};$ $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ decomposes on heating to the oxide and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ at $1080^{\circ}C$ $\mathrm{CO}_{2}\sim 1\mathrm{mm}$ press.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Hydroxides, react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.   
(iii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e., react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Potassium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.

References [18-23]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 126.3. Corrosion studies from primary research literature   

<table><tr><td colspan="2">Studies</td><td>References</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="5">A</td><td>Stainless steel</td><td>[24]</td></tr><tr><td>Acid-base relationships in molten carbonates</td><td>[25-28]</td></tr><tr><td>Hydrolysis (H2O) reactions</td><td>[29-31]</td></tr><tr><td>Corrosion in molten salts, annotated biblio.</td><td>[32]</td></tr><tr><td>Molten carbonates: fuel cells, thermal energy storage, and coal gasification, stack gases</td><td>[24,33,37]</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="7">B</td><td>Ni, Armco Fe, steels</td><td>[38-40]</td></tr><tr><td>Fe (effects of H2O)</td><td>[41]</td></tr><tr><td>Pt, Ag and alloys</td><td>[42-44]</td></tr><tr><td>Thermodynamics of corrosion</td><td>[45-48]</td></tr><tr><td>Annotated corrosion biblio.</td><td>[32]</td></tr><tr><td>Electrochemical aspects</td><td>[49]</td></tr><tr><td>Reviews</td><td>[46,50-52]</td></tr></table>

Compatibility studies: A: principally molten $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ ; B: principally molten KOH. No compatibility studies found specifically for molten mixtures of $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ -KOH.

References [24-52]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}: 898^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Li2SO4:859°C

Eutectic 1, melting point:

$470^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $60 \, \text{mol} \, \% \, \text{Li}_2 \text{SO}_4$ ; $26.5 \, \text{mol} \, \% \, \text{Li}_2 \text{CO}_3$ ; $13.5 \, \text{mol} \, \% \, \text{K}_2 \text{SO}_4$

Eutectic 2, melting point:

$562^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $55 \, \mathrm{mol} \%$ $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}$ ; $35 \, \mathrm{mol} \%$ $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ ; $10 \, \mathrm{mol} \%$ $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$

Peritectic melting point:

565°C, composition: 65.83 mol % K₂SO₄; 34.17 mol % Li₂CO₃

Reciprocal Salt Pair Reaction:

$\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4} + \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4} + \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3} + 5.3 \, \text{kcal equiv}^{-1}$

![](images/c0544eb6c005898ebee954e2d89de053932581d7ddf77b33a7ead27dbf36b5cd.jpg)  
Figure 127.1. Phase diagram for Li, K/CO₃, SO₄ system. System K₂CO₃-Li₂SO₄ is the diagonal as marked; note the reciprocal salt pair reaction

$\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4} + \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3} = \mathrm{K}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4} + \mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{CO}_{3} + 5.3 \, \text{kcal equiv}^{-1}$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ , classified as strongly caustic; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ ; the toxic qualities are those of the cation, lithium.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ (m.pt. $898^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) decomposes on heating to the oxide and oxygen.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns). Potassium carbonate itself is classified as a strong caustic.   
(iii) Sulfates, when heated to decomposition, decompose, evolving $\mathrm{SO}_3$ , which in turn dissociates to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$ (see above); toxic, corrosive fumes.

References [18-24].

(127) $\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3} - \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$

```txt
System 128 Na3AlF6-LiF 
```

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

LiF: 848°C

Eutectic melting point:

694°C, composition: 15.0 mol % Na₃AlF₆

![](images/0ab35902b7afe691ca05c3cdce8b621a9fcaba1cf3827f2b85a0756bb9f53483.jpg)

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [24]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \quad (1 2 8. 1)
$$

precision: in table 128.1

uncertainty: $\sim$ 1.5%

References [27,28]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ at its m.pt. $(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; LiF, at its m.pt. $(848^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 8.8 \times 10^{-3}\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting point:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

NaF: 995°C

Eutectic melting point:

888°C, composition: 60 mol % Na₃AlF₆

![](images/bcc0c553eb202c300ccec80f552f5d8b345041eaa1fdb02a2c9f1e23d7a2e09b.jpg)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [26]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {129.1}
$$

precision: in table 129.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: Na₃AlF₆ at m.pt., 1010°C, ∼ << 0.5mm; NaF at m.pt., 995°C, ∼ 0.5mm.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [28-33]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

KF: 856°C

Eutectic 1, melting point:

890°C, composition: 55.9 mol % KF
25 wt % KF

Eutectic 2, melting point:

$740^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 91.9 mol % KF
75.7 wt % KF

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: Na₃AlF₆, at m.pt. 1000°C, ∼ << 0.5mm, KF, at m.pt., 856°C, ∼ 0.5mm.

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salts bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [21-26]

References [15]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic; toxicity of berylium compounds is rated high (i.e. may cause death or permanent injury after very short exposure to small quantities).   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ , at m.pt. $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \sim < 0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ ; $\mathrm{BeF}_{2}$ , at m.pt. $797^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \sim < 0.5 \mathrm{~mm}$ .

# B. Disaster Hazards

(i) Molten salts bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [16-21]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{MgF}_{2}$ : 1263°C

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

Eutectic melting point:

$924^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $57.5 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ ; [82.0 wt $\%$ , $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}]$

References [1-23]

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [24]

Equation:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \rho = 2. 0 9 6 + 1. 7 2 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} C \tag {132.1} \\ (C = \text {mol} \% \text {MgF} _ {2}) \\ \end{array}
$$

precision: not estimated, graphical data.

uncertainty: $\sim$ 5.0%

$$

No data

4. Viscosity (η)

Measurement method: oscillating sphere technique [25]

Equation: (viscosity-composition isotherm)

$$
\eta = a + b C + c C ^ {2} \tag {132.2}
$$

$$
\left(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{mol}\% \mathrm{MgF}_{2}\right)
$$

precision: in table 132.3 uncertainty: $\sim$ $15\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [29] and $\mathrm{MgF}_{2}$ [29].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [27-32]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ : $1010^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ : $1418^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

$946^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $50 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ ; [72.9 wt $\%$ Na $_{3}$ AlF $_{6}$ ]

# (133) $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6} - \mathrm{CaF}_{2}$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [30], and $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ [30].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [28-33].

# 7. Corrosion

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

BaF 2 : 1320°C

Eutectic melting point:

835°C composition: 62.5 wt % BaF₂ (67 mol % BaF₂)

![](images/d5a5b3679b8b270e811495d897920d825affd841920e740d25a3066bf3890d3d.jpg)

References [1-17]

2. Density (ρ)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [18]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {134.1}
$$

precision: in table 134.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$

5. Electrical Conductance (K) No data

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathsf{BaF}_2$ , at m.pt., $1320^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , $\sim < 0.5\mathsf{mm}$ ; $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{AlF}_6$ , at m.p.t., $1010^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , $\sim << 0.5\mathsf{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contracted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [20-25]

$$
\text {S y s t e m} 1 3 5 \quad \mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {A l F} _ {6} - \mathrm {A l F} _ {3}
$$

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

A1F3: does not melt; sublimes with 1 atm equal press. at $\sim$ $1255^{\circ}C$ .

Eutectic melting point:

737°C, composition: 30 wt % AlF₃

$694^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $39 \mathrm{wt} \% \mathrm{AlF}_{3}$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic; alumina is classed as a nuisance particulate   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information on this system; Na₃AlF₆ at its m.pt. (1010°C), << 0.5mm, AlF₃ sublimes at ~ 1260°C.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [29-34]

References [30]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, very low; Na $_3$ AlF $_6$ , inorganic fluorides are generally irritant and toxic

(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl, at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; Na $_3$ AlF $_6$ ,

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Inorganic halides when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.

References [31-30]

(136) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ -NaCl

8. Diffusion

Measurement method: rotating disc electrode [81]

List of diffusing species investigated in $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ -NaCl as solvent $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$

precision: \* uncertainty: $\sim \pm 20\%$ *insufficient data for estimate

Equation: (isothermal study at 1353K)

$$
D = 1. 3 0 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} + 5. 7 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} W \tag {136.4}
$$

where W is the weight fraction of NaCl in the $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ -NaCl mixture

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

BaCl2: 962°C

Eutectic 1, melting point:

$745^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $\sim 65 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$

Eutectic 2, melting point:

$762^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $\sim 91 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$

Compound melting point:

$786^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $80 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ (i.e., $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6} . 4 \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ )

![](images/d19cb47a6a02604723cb21220a557eaff268f0affca90a80802f7e4b633d175a.jpg)

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic, soluble barium compounds are classed as toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: Na₂AlF₆ at m.pt., 1010°C, ∅ < 0.5mm; BaCl₂, at m.pt., 962°C, ∅ < 0.5mm.

# B. Disaster hazard

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides and chlorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [21-26]

# 7. Corrosion

B: used largely in fluorides physical properties measurements; C: technological aspects, in aluminum reduction cells; D: more general studies, basic principles, and surveys.

References [27-71]

(137) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}-\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$

8. Diffusion

No diffusion studies reported. For a related but more complex system, i.e., $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6} - \mathrm{AlF}_{3} - \mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ , a limited chronopotentiometric study of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$ has been reported [72]. $\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{Cu}^{+}}$ x $10^{5}$ (cm $^{2}$ s $^{-1}$ ) was reported as 2.8 ( $\sim$ 15%) at 1023K.

References [72]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

B2O3: 450°C

The solubility of $\mathsf{B}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ in molten cryolite appears unlimited [1].

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic; alumina is classed as a nuisance particulate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information on this system; Na₃AlF₆ at its m.pt. (1010°C), << 0.5mm.

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions", i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

$\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ : $1010^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ : $2040^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

E. $960 - 963^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $18.6 - 21.1 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

Measurement method: in table 139.8

Equations:

$$
\begin{array}{l} (\text {c o n d u c t a n c e - t e m p e r a t u r e d e p e n d e n c e}) (34) \\ \kappa = a + b T + c T ^ {2} (139.4) \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\text {(c o n d u c t a n c e - c o m p o s i t i o n i s o t h e r m)} \tag {35}
$$

$$
\kappa = a ^ {\prime} + b ^ {\prime} C + c ^ {\prime} C ^ {2} \tag {139.5}
$$

$$
(C = \text {mol} \% \text {Al} _ {2} \text {O} _ {3})
$$

precision: in table 139.9

uncertainty: in table 139.8

References [5,34,35,40]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic; alumina is classed as a nuisance particulate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information on this system; Na₃AlF₆ at its m.pt. (1000°C), << 0.5mm.

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [41-46].

Thonstad [95] reported a value of $1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^2 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ for $\mathsf{D}_{\mathsf{Al}_2\mathsf{O}_3}$ at $1293\text{K}$ , and the diffusion coefficient was constant (within limits of experimental error ( $\pm 15\%$ ) over the investigated concentration range (0.25-12 wt% $\mathsf{Al}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ ). At $1353\text{K}$ , Shurygin et al. [96,100] reported a value of $1.33 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{cm}^2 \, \text{s}^{-1}$ for $\mathsf{D}_{\mathsf{Al}_2\mathsf{O}_3}$ .

# 5. Electrical Conductance (K)

Measurement method: classical ac technique [2]

Equation:

$$
\kappa = a + b T + c T ^ {2} + d T ^ {3} \tag {140.1}
$$

precision: in table 140.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 30\%$

References [2]

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure, no data.   
B. Disaster hazards   
(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [17-21]

$775 \pm 3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $\sim 1.5 \, \text{mol} \% \, \text{Al}_{2} \text{O}_{3}$

$$

(141) $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information on this system; Li3AlF6 at its m.pt., $\sim 785^{\circ}C$ , $\sim < 5mm$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [21-26]

(142) LiOH-LiNO3

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: LiOH, very caustic and toxic; $\mathrm{LiNO}_3$ , moderate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: LiOH, at m.pt. $(460^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $<< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; LiNO₃ (m.pt. $253^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) decomposes with heating to the nitrite and oxygen just above its m.pt.

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": violent generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; sudden explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Hydroxides react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.   
(iii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \rightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {I} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {142.3}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \rightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {142.4}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite conversion (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

In the temperature range $550 - 660^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and under oxygen, the conversion of $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ to $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ goes to completion; between $650 - 750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two salts interconvert (see above), $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ becoming increasingly unstable; above $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the nitrite decomposition: $2\mathrm{KNO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{N}_2 + 3 / _2\mathrm{O}_2$ goes to completion.

(iv) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , permitted as a food additive; NaOH, very caustic and is corrosive to all body tissues.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system, but see $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ [23], and NaOH [23].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {143.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {143.2}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.   
(iv) Dangerous; with water or steam, reacts with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very strongly caustic; attacks living tissue.

References [17-24]

$\mathrm{E}_{2}: 220^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: $68 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ KOH

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathsf{KNO}_3$ , permitted as a food additive; KOH, highly caustic and is very corrosive to all body tissue   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system, but see $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ [73], and KOH [68]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": violent generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; sudden explosive expansion of "trapped" air   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {144.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {144.2}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

In the temperature range $550 - 600^{\circ}C$ , and under oxygen, the conversion of $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ to $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ goes to completion; between $650 - 750^{\circ}C$ , the two salts interconvert (see above), $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ becoming increasingly unstable; above $800^{\circ}C$ , the nitrite decomposition: $2\mathrm{KNO}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{N}_2 + 3 / _2\mathrm{O}_2$ goes to completion

(iii) Nitrates are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous   
(iv) Hydroxides, dangerous; react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very strongly caustic; attacks living tissue

References [16-23]

2. Density (p)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [19]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b T \tag {145.1}
$$

precision: in table 145.1

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

Table 145.1. Parameters of equation (145.1) and precisions

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , permitted as a food additive; $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ , permitted in food; there appears some implication of increased cancer with chronic ingestion of nitrites.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ [32], and $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ [33]

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {145.6}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {145.7}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iii) Nitrates and nitrites are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [23-33]

$$
C _ {p} = 6 1. 5 0 - 3 5. 3 3 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} T \tag {145.8}
$$

precision: $\sim \pm 1.5\%$

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 10\%$

(B) Diagonal Pair $\mathrm{KNO}_2\text{-NaNO}_3$

This system is a diagonal in the system: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2 - \mathrm{NaNO}_3 - \mathrm{KNO}_3 - \mathrm{KNO}_2$ ; for the ternary system: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2 - \mathrm{NaNO}_3 - \mathrm{KNO}_3$ , see system 148.

3. Surface Tension (Y) No data   
4. Viscosity (n) No data   
5. Electrical Conductance (K) No data   
6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , permitted as a food additive; $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ toxicity rating, severe; there appears some implication of increased cancer with chronic ingestion of nitrates.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ [53] and $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ [54].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates and nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {146.3}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {146.4}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iii) Nitrates and nitrites are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [21-32]

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , permitted as a food additive; $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ , permitted in food; there appears some implication of increased cancer with chronic ingestion of nitrites   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ [74], and $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ [75]

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped air".   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
\mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {147.4}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \tag {147.5}
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

In the temperature range $550 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and under oxygen, the conversion of $\mathrm{KNO}_2$ to $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ goes to completion; between $650 - 750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the two salts interconvert (see above), $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ becoming increasingly unstable; above $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the nitrite decomposition: $2\mathrm{KNO}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{N}_2 + 3 / 2\mathrm{O}_2$ goes to completion.

Nitrates and nitrites are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [22-29]

13. Thermal Conductivity (liquid) $\left( {\lambda }_{\ell }\right)$

Measurement method: transient method [50,51]

$$
\lambda = 1. 5 0 1 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3} - 0. 5 9 0 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6} \mathrm {T} \tag {147.6}
$$

precision: $\sim \pm 1.0\%$ uncertainty: $\sim \pm 10\%$

Table 147.8. Thermal conductivity of melt from equation (147.6)   

<table><tr><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td><td>T(K)</td><td>λ x 104(cal cm-1s-1K-1)</td></tr><tr><td>455</td><td>12.3</td><td>530</td><td>11.9</td></tr><tr><td>460</td><td>12.3</td><td>540</td><td>11.8</td></tr><tr><td>470</td><td>12.2</td><td>550</td><td>11.8</td></tr><tr><td>480</td><td>12.2</td><td>560</td><td>11.7</td></tr><tr><td>490</td><td>12.1</td><td>570</td><td>11.7</td></tr><tr><td>500</td><td>12.1</td><td>580</td><td>11.6</td></tr><tr><td>510</td><td>12.0</td><td>590</td><td>11.5</td></tr><tr><td>520</td><td>11.9</td><td>600</td><td>11.5</td></tr></table>

Small amounts of water ( $\sim$ 5 mol $\%$ ) were found to increase the thermal conductivity in the vicinity of the melting point i.e. $\sim$ $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by $\sim$ $3\%$ , and appeared to have no effect at somewhat higher temperatures (i.e. $\sim$ $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).

The thermal conductivity could be increased by $\sim 20\%$ at $250^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by adding about $10\mathrm{mol}\%$ KF; this is close to the saturation solubility, and KF may deposit on cooling.

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ , permitted in food; $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ , $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ , permitted as food additives, there appears some implication of increased cancer incidence with chronic ingestion of nitrites.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ [25], $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ [26], and $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ [26]

# (148) $\mathrm{NaNO}_2 - \mathrm{NaNO}_3 - \mathrm{KNO}_3$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) On decomposition, nitrates and nitrites emit toxic fumes (oxides of nitrogen) viz:

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {3} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} + \mathrm {O} _ {2} \tag {148.4.1}
$$

$$
2 \mathrm {M N O} _ {2} \longrightarrow \mathrm {M} _ {2} \mathrm {O} + \mathrm {N O} + \mathrm {N O} _ {2} \quad (1 4 8. 4. 2)
$$

The subsequent decomposition reactions are complex; if the gas phase is continuously removed, the nitrite decomposition (above) to NO and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is dominant.

(iii) Nitrates and nitrites are powerful oxidizing agents; violent (explosive) reactions possible in molten nitrates and carbonaceous materials (organic cpds., oils, carbon,...); aluminum alloys and bath sludges (e.g. iron oxides); magnesium alloys. Dangerous.

References [19-29]

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: sulfur, low; $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}$ , variable [18].

(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{S}$ , no data; sulfur; at m.pt. (115°C), $\sim 0.03\mathrm{mm}$ ; at $185^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 1\mathrm{mm}$ ; at $444.6^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 1$ atm (b.pt.).

(149) $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}$

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten sulfides and polysulfides may react violently (explosively) with strong oxidants: $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ evolved on contact with moisture/water can form explosive mixtures with air; $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ , highly toxic.   
(ii) Sulfides and polysulfides when heated to decomposition, or contacted with water, steam, acids, produce highly toxic fumes (oxides of sulfur, $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ ). Dangerous.   
(iii) Sulfur burns with the formation of oxides of sulfur $(\mathrm{SO}_2; \mathrm{SO}_3)$ ; toxic and corrosive fumes.

References [16-21]

7. Corrosion

Table 149.1 Corrosion studies from primary research literature

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: sulfur, low; $\mathrm{K}_2\mathrm{S}$ , variable [56].   
(ii) Vapor pressure: K₂S, no data; sulfur; at m.µt (115°C), ~ 0.03mm; at 185°C, ~ 1mm; at 444.6°C, ~ 1 atm (b.µt.).

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten sulfides and polysulfides may react violently (explosively) with strong oxidants: $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ evolved on contact with moisture/water can form explosive mixtures with air; $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{S}$ , highly toxic.   
(ii) Sulfides and polysulfides when heated to decomposition, or contacted with water, steam, acids, produce highly toxic fumes (oxides of sulfur, $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{S}$ ). Dangerous.   
(iii) Sulfur burns with the formation of oxides of sulfur $(\mathrm{SO}_2; \mathrm{SO}_3)$ ; toxic and corrosive fumes.

References [17-22].

# References [17]

# 5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: NaCl, permitted in foods; NaOH, strongly caustic and toxic; $\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}$ , moderate, classified as caustic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: NaCl, at m.pt. $(800^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\sim 0.34\mathrm{mm}$ ; NaOH, at m.pt. $(318^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $\ll 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ (m.pt. $858^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) decomposes on heating to the oxide and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ; $\mathrm{CO}_2$ pressure at $950^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\sim 1\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over: and/or equipment failure;; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Chlorides, when heated to decomposition or contacted with acids, highly toxic chloride fumes are evolved.   
(iii) Hydroxides react with water or steam with evolution of heat; the aqueous solution is very caustic and attacks living tissue; dangerous.   
(iv) Carbonates, when heated with $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ pressures less than equilibrium dissociation pressures, decompose to form alkali metal oxides; the toxicology and disaster hazards are the same as those of very caustic solutions, i.e. react with water or steam (exothermic) to form solutions that are markedly aggressive to all body tissues (chemical burns).

References [18-23]

# 7. Corrosion

Table 151.5. Corrosion studies from primary research literature

Compatibility studies: A: molten carbonates, principally $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ ; B: molten hydroxides, principally $\mathrm{NaOH}$ ; C: molten chlorides, principally $\mathrm{NaCl}$ . No compatibility studies found specifically for molten $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ - $\mathrm{NaOH}-\mathrm{NaCl}$ mixtures.

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{\hat{m}})$

Pure substance melting points:

LiF: 848°C

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

A12O3: 2040°C

Eutectic melting point:

697°C, composition 59.8 mol % Na₃AlF₆; 4.5 mol % Al₂O₃; 35.7 mol % LiF

56.5 wt $\%$ Na $_3$ AlF $_6$ ; 2.0 wt $\%$ Al $_2$ O $_3$ ; 41.5 wt $\%$ LiF

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.

(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [24] and NaF [28].

(152) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{LiF}$

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosion": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [22-28]

(153) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.

(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [22] and $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ [22].

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.

(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [20-25].

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

A12O3: 2040°C

$\mathrm{AlF}_{3}$ : does not melt; sublimes with 1 atm equal press. at $\sim 1255^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Eutectic melting point:

E 1: $684^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 67.3 wt % Na 3 Al F 6, 4.4 wt % Al 2 O 3

37.4 mol % Na₃AlF₆, 4.1 mol % Al₂O₃

$\mathrm{P}_{1}$ : $723^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , composition: 59.5 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ , 3.2 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

45.6 mol % Na₃AlF₆, 5.8 mol % Al₂O₃

![](images/1ac285362818ba4adc8eff00f765fd0911acfedb4d0576e6906275d3787d8b3d.jpg)

4. Viscosity (n)

Measurement method: oscillation technique [22]

Equation:

$$
\eta = a + b T + c T ^ {2} + d T ^ {3} \tag {154.1}
$$

precision: in table 154.2

uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2\%$

References [22]

(154) $\mathrm{Na}_{3} \mathrm{AlF}_{6}-\mathrm{AlF}_{3}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally irritants and toxic; alumina is classed as a nuisance particulate.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ at m.pt. $(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , $<< 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ ; $\mathrm{AlF}_{3}$ at its m.pt. $(1291^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ , no data; sublimes at $\sim 1240^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .   
B. Disaster hazards   
(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [23-28]

1. Melting Temperatures $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

NaCl: $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

A12O3: 2040°C

Eutectic melting point:

735°C, composition: < 1 wt % Al₂O₃, ~ 31.5 wt % Na₃AlF₆, ~ 68.5 wt % NaCl

![](images/081372847116ad98a7b6720991c78d8984f1f179db51f020c3760b6538567ac5.jpg)

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

# 6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.

(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [25] and NaCl [29].

# B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides and chlorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [23-29]

1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

<table><tr><td>Na3AlF6:</td><td>1010°C</td></tr><tr><td>Al2O3:</td><td>2040°C</td></tr><tr><td>Li3AlF6:</td><td>785°C</td></tr></table>

Eutectic melting point:

760°C composition: ~ 27 mol % Na₃AlF₆; ~ 65 mol % Li₃AlF₆; ~ 8 mol % Al₂O₃

Reaction point:

693°C composition: ~ 33 mol % Na₃AlF₆ ~ 63 mol % Li₃AlF₆ ~ 4 mol % Al₂O₃

Peritectic melting point:

683°C composition: ~ 33 mol % Na₃AlF₆ ~ 61 mol % Li₃AlF₆ ~ 6 mol % Al₂O₃

5. Electrical Conductance (K)

No data

6. Safety and Hazards

A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information for this system; but see $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [18] and $\mathrm{Li}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ [18].

B. Disaster hazards

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e. explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [16-21]

# 1. Melting Temperature $(T_{m})$

Pure substance melting points:

Na3AlF6: 1010°C

$\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ : $2040^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{SiO}_2$ : quartz: $1610^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ tridymite: $1680^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ cristobalite: $1728^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$

Invariant points:

The large solubility of $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ and $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ at the invariant points (69 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ , 14 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ at $1010^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and 50 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ , 17 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) imply substantial solubilities of the aluminum silicates in this system. The restricted field of liquid at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ indicates that the liquidus does not extend much below this temperature [2].

Figure 157.1. Phase diagram of isothermal section at $1010^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6} - \mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3} - \mathrm{SiO}_{2}$   
![](images/103ea560af37abfdc9e9a5e68ad6cee285498efcd2ee5039d3056d60c065caec.jpg)  
(C: corundum; T: tridymite; L: liquid)

\mathrm {N a} _ {3} \mathrm {A l F} _ {6} - \mathrm {A l} _ {2} \mathrm {O} _ {3} - \mathrm {S i O} _ {2} \tag {157}

2. Density (p)

Measurement method: Archimedean technique [16]

Equation:

$$
\rho = a + b C \quad (1 5 7. 1)
$$

(C = \text {mol} \% \text {Al} _ {2} \text {O} _ {3})
$$

precision: in table 157.1 uncertainty: $\sim \pm 2\%$

6. Safety and Hazards

# A. Hazard rating

(i) Toxicity: inorganic fluorides are generally quite irritant and toxic.   
(ii) Vapor pressure: no information on this system; $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{AlF}_{6}$ at its m.pt. (1010°C), $\sim \ll 0.5\mathrm{mm}$ .

# B. Disaster hazard

(i) Molten salt bath "explosions": i.e., explosive generation of steam due to bulk water "carry-over" and/or equipment failure; i.e., explosive expansion of "trapped" air.   
(ii) Fluorides, when heated to decomposition, or contacted with acids, emit highly toxic fumes.

References [17-22]

B: used largely in fluorides physical properties measurements; C: technological aspects, in aluminum reduction cells; D: more general studies, basic principles, and surveys.

□ Document describes a computer program; SF-185, FIPS Software Summary, is attached.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="4">11. ABSTRACT (A 200-word or less factual summary of most significant information. If document includes a significant
bibliography or literature survey, mention it here)
The present work provides selected data with value judgements for an additional
set of 107 salt systems of interest as candidate materials for thermal energy storage
sub-systems, for electrochemical energy storage systems, and in electrochemical aluminum
production. The physical properties assessed are: melting points; phase diagrams;
eutectic compositions; density; surface tension; viscosity; electrical conductivity;
diffusion constants for ions; heat of fusion; heat capacity; volume change on fusion;
vapor pressure; thermal conductivity (liquid and solid); and cryoscopic constant. The
status of corrosion studies in the form of annotated bibliographic summaries, and
salient observations on safety and hazards are also reported. A summarizing series of
tables is provided as index to the data-gaps status for this set of candidate materials.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="4">12. KEY WORDS (Six to twelve entries; alphabetical order; capitalize only proper names; and separate key words by semicolons)
Corrosion; data compilations; electrochemical aluminum production; electrochemical energy
storage materials; molten salts; physical properties; safety and hazards; thermal energy
storage materials; thermal properties; thermodynamic properties; transport properties.</td></tr><tr><td rowspan="2" colspan="3">13. AVAILABILITY
Unlimited

# Subscribe now—The new National Bureau of Standards Journal

The expanded Journal of Research of the National Bureau of Standards reports NBS research and development in those disciplines of the physical and engineering sciences in which the Bureau is active. These include physics, chemistry, engineering, mathematics, and computer sciences. Papers cover a broad range of subjects, with major emphasis on measurement methodology, and the basic technology underlying standardization. Also included from time to time are survey articles on topics closely related to the Bureau's technical and scientific programs. As a special service to subscribers each issue contains complete citations to all recent NBS publications in NBS and non-NBS media. Issued six times a year. Annual subscriptions: domestic $13.; foreign$ 16.25. Single copy, $3. domestic; $3.75 foreign.

Note: The Journal was formerly published in two sections: Section A "Physics and Chemistry" and Section B "Mathematical Sciences."

NBS Board of Editors

Executive Editor (Mathematics)

Andrew J. Fowell (Engineering)

Joseph O. Harrison (Computer Science)

Howard J. M. Hanley (Boulder Labs.)

# NBS TECHNICAL PUBLICATIONS

# PERIODICALS

JOURNAL OF RESEARCH—The Journal of Research of the National Bureau of Standards reports NBS research and development in those disciplines of the physical and engineering sciences in which the Bureau is active. These include physics, chemistry, engineering, mathematics, and computer sciences. Papers cover a broad range of subjects, with major emphasis on measurement methodology and the basic technology underlying standardization. Also included from time to time are survey articles on topics closely related to the Bureau's technical and scientific programs. As a special service to subscribers each issue contains complete citations to all recent Bureau publications in both NBS and non-NBS media. Issued six times a year. Annual subscription: domestic $13; foreign$ 16.25. Single copy, $3 domestic; $3.75 foreign.

NOTE: The Journal was formerly published in two sections: Section A "Physics and Chemistry" and Section B "Mathematical Sciences."

DIMENSIONS/NBS—This monthly magazine is published to inform scientists, engineers, business and industry leaders, teachers, students, and consumers of the latest advances in science and technology, with primary emphasis on work at NBS. The magazine highlights and reviews such issues as energy research, fire protection, building technology, metric conversion, pollution abatement, health and safety, and consumer product performance. In addition, it reports the results of Bureau programs in measurement standards and techniques, properties of matter and materials, engineering standards and services, instrumentation, and automatic data processing. Annual subscription: domestic $11; foreign $13.75.

# NONPERIODICALS

Monographs—Major contributions to the technical literature on various subjects related to the Bureau's scientific and technical activities.

Handbooks—Recommended codes of engineering and industrial practice (including safety codes) developed in cooperation with interested industries, professional organizations, and regulatory bodies.

Special Publications—Include proceedings of conferences sponsored by NBS, NBS annual reports, and other special publications appropriate to this grouping such as wall charts, pocket cards, and bibliographies.

Applied Mathematics Series—Mathematical tables, manuals, and studies of special interest to physicists, engineers, chemists, biologists, mathematicians, computer programmers, and others engaged in scientific and technical work.

National Standard Reference Data Series—Provides quantitative data on the physical and chemical properties of materials, compiled from the world's literature and critically evaluated. Developed under a worldwide program coordinated by NBS under the authority of the National Standard Data Act (Public Law 90-396).

NOTE: The principal publication outlet for the foregoing data is the Journal of Physical and Chemical Reference Data (JPCRD) published quarterly for NBS by the American Chemical Society (ACS) and the American Institute of Physics (AIP). Subscriptions, reprints, and supplements available from ACS, 1155 Sixteenth St., NW, Washington, DC 20056.

Building Science Series—Disseminates technical information developed at the Bureau on building materials, components, systems, and whole structures. The series presents research results, test methods, and performance criteria related to the structural and environmental functions and the durability and safety characteristics of building elements and systems.

Technical Notes—Studies or reports which are complete in themselves but restrictive in their treatment of a subject. Analogous to monographs but not so comprehensive in scope or definitive in treatment of the subject area. Often serve as a vehicle for final reports of work performed at NBS under the sponsorship of other government agencies.

Voluntary Product Standards—Developed under procedures published by the Department of Commerce in Part 10, Title 15, of the Code of Federal Regulations. The standards establish nationally recognized requirements for products, and provide all concerned interests with a basis for common understanding of the characteristics of the products. NBS administers this program as a supplement to the activities of the private sector standardizing organizations.

Consumer Information Series—Practical information, based on NBS research and experience, covering areas of interest to the consumer. Easily understandable language and illustrations provide useful background knowledge for shopping in today's technological marketplace.

Order the above NBS publications from: Superintendent of Documents, Government Printing Office, Washington, DC 20402.

Order the following NBS publications—FIPS and NBSIR's—from the National Technical Information Services, Springfield, VA 22161.

Federal Information Processing Standards Publications (FIPS PUB)—Publications in this series collectively constitute the Federal Information Processing Standards Register. The Register serves as the official source of information in the Federal Government regarding standards issued by NBS pursuant to the Federal Property and Administrative Services Act of 1949 as amended, Public Law 89-306 (79 Stat. 1127), and as implemented by Executive Order 11717 (38 FR 12315, dated May 11, 1973) and Part 6 of Title 15 CFR (Code of Federal Regulations).

NBS Interagency Reports (NBSIR)—A special series of interim or final reports on work performed by NBS for outside sponsors (both government and non-government). In general, initial distribution is handled by the sponsor; public distribution is by the National Technical Information Services, Springfield, VA 22161, in paper copy or microfiche form.

OFFICIAL BUSINESS

Penalty for Private Use, $300
